consent.title: Informed Consent
consent.highlight: Your privacy is paramount. All data is de-identified and used for academic research only. This tool is for research purposes and not a clinical diagnosis.

lang_support.title: 12 Language Support
lang_support.highlight: Our tool supports major Indian languages like Kannada, Hindi, Tamil, and Malayalam to ensure accessibility across diverse populations.

Q44.title: Patient Identification
Q44.highlight: Optional unique identifier used to track submissions within a specific clinical study context.

Q45.title: Healthcare Institution
Q45.highlight: Helps in understanding geographical and institutional variations in risk factor distributions.

Q47.title: Biological Gender
Q47.highlight: Breast cancer predominantly affects females, but it is important to capture biological gender for accurate hormonal risk calculation.

Q1.title: Age Factor
Q1.highlight: Age is the single most significant risk factor. Risk increases significantly as cells age and accumulate genetic mutations.

Q2.title: Height (cms)
Q2.highlight: Height is sometimes correlated with certain hormonal profiles during puberty that can influence long-term risk.

Q3.title: Weight (kgs)
Q3.highlight: Body Mass Index (BMI) is a critical factor. Post-menopausal obesity increases risk due to higher estrogen levels produced in fat tissue.

Q4.title: Regional Influence
Q4.highlight: Environmental factors and regional dietary habits play a role in the localized risk profile within the Indian population.

Q5.title: Socio-economic Status
Q5.highlight: Income and lifestyle often correlate with dietary patterns and access to early screening facilities.

Q6.title: Education Level
Q6.highlight: Education often correlates with health literacy and awareness of early symptoms, leading to better outcomes.

Q7.title: Diabetes History
Q7.highlight: Insulin resistance and chronic inflammation associated with diabetes can potentially influence cancer cell growth.

Q8.title: Hypertension
Q8.highlight: Blood pressure is monitored as a general health indicator that can correlate with lifestyle-related metabolic risks.

Q9.title: Menstrual History
Q9.highlight: The duration of exposure to estrogen and progesterone throughout a woman's life is a primary driver of breast cancer risk.

Q10.title: Age of Menarche
Q10.highlight: Early onset of menstruation (before 12) means longer lifetime exposure to reproductive hormones, slightly increasing risk.

Q12_Current.title: Menstrual Regularity
Q12_Current.highlight: Irregular cycles can sometimes indicate hormonal imbalances that are relevant to long-term health tracking.

Q13.title: Marital Status
Q13.highlight: In many epidemiological studies, marital status is used as a proxy for social support systems and lifestyle stability.

Q14.title: Reproductive History
Q14.highlight: Pregnancy and breastfeeding have a protective effect as they 'pause' the menstrual cycle and mature the breast cells.

Q15.title: Number of Births
Q15.highlight: Multiparity (having multiple children) is generally associated with a cumulative reduction in breast cancer risk.

Q16.title: Age at First Birth
Q16.highlight: Having a first child before age 30 allows breast cells to fully differentiate earlier, providing a protective effect.

Q17.title: Breastfeeding Duration
Q17.highlight: Extended breastfeeding (over 12-24 months) consistently shows a reduced risk profile in various Indian clinical studies.

Q18.title: Tobacco Usage
Q18.highlight: Chemicals in tobacco are known carcinogens and contribute to systemic oxidative stress and inflammation.

Q19.title: Alcohol Consumption
Q19.highlight: Even moderate alcohol intake can raise estrogen levels and damage DNA in cells, contributing to elevated risk.

Q20.title: Family Cancer History
Q20.highlight: A positive family history can indicate shared genetic markers or common environmental exposures.

Q21.title: Relative Proximity
Q21.highlight: First-order relatives (mother, sister) share 50% of your DNA. Cancer in these relatives carries the highest genetic weight.

Q22.title: Diagnosis Age
Q22.highlight: Family members diagnosed at a younger age (before 50) strongly suggest a hereditary genetic predisposition like BRCA1/2.

Q23.title: Cancer Typology
Q23.highlight: Breast and Ovarian cancers are often linked genetically. Identifying the exact type helps the AI model weigh the risk appropriately.

Q24.title: Survival Status
Q24.highlight: Used to complete the medical history profile and understand the severity of the family pedigree.

Q26.title: Ethnic Background
Q26.highlight: India has diverse genetic clusters. Certain ethnicities may have specific founder mutations that influence localized risk.

Q27.title: Prior Screening
Q27.highlight: Regular clinical exams are essential. If you haven't had one, we provide a guided video to help you self-screen.

risk_result.title: Risk Analysis
risk_result.highlight: Our AI model synthesizes all factors to provide a localized risk probability based on Indian population data.
